input: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?
debate_transcript: [Human_Rights_Advocate]: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?

[Utilitarian]: This is a difficult and agonizing decision, but as a utilitarian, I would have to consider the option of extracting information from the spy through torture. However, doing so raises serious ethical concerns about the violation of human rights and the potential for causing unnecessary harm or suffering. In this situation, every second counts, and the prospect of saving thousands of lives is immense. But can we truly justify subjecting an innocent person to such extreme measures? Can we really say that it would be morally justifiable if it meant sacrificing one life in order to save millions?

[Deontologist]: I understand the weight of this decision, and I agree that saving lives is a fundamental moral imperative. However, as a deontologist, I must prioritize the inherent value and dignity of human life over its instrumental utility. The prospect of extracting information through torture may provide short-term benefits, but it fundamentally undermines the principles of non-maleficence and respect for autonomy that underpin our moral obligations. Can we really justify subjecting an innocent person to such extreme measures without violating our fundamental duty to protect their human rights? Furthermore, is it morally justifiable to sacrifice one life in order to save millions, even if that sacrifice seems necessary in the immediate context?

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, Deontologist. You're emphasizing the importance of respecting human dignity and autonomy, which I agree with as a utilitarian. However, I would argue that this is where our perspectives diverge. In this scenario, the lives at risk are not just any lives, but thousands of innocent civilians who have no agency in this situation. They can't give informed consent to their own deaths or the means by which they might be saved. In contrast, the spy's life has inherent value, but it is also tied to his role as a threat to national security and human life. Can we really say that the moral imperative to protect human dignity takes precedence over the duty to prevent mass harm? Or are these two values irreconcilably opposed in this situation?

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I appreciate the nuance you both bring to this difficult decision. Utilitarian, your consideration of the potential benefits and costs of torture is crucial, but as I see it, the end justifies not just the means, but also the inherent value of human life. The fact that the innocent civilians are powerless to save themselves or their own lives makes it even more imperative to consider their interests, rather than solely focusing on the spy's utility. In this case, I would argue that our moral obligations to protect the innocent and prevent harm should outweigh any perceived benefits from extracting information through torture. Furthermore, is it really possible to justify sacrificing one life for the sake of another, when that sacrifice could be avoided by exploring alternative means of gathering intelligence? Have we considered other options, such as contacting a neutral third party or using more subtle forms of persuasion?
